ref: When your father said the speckled sheep would be my wages, all of them were speckled. And when he said the spotted ones would be mine, all of them were spotted.
text: Ne egblɔ bena: lã ŋɔtaŋɔtawo nanye wò fetu la, ekema lãha blibo la katã dzia vi ŋɔtaŋɔtawo sɔŋ, eye ne egagblɔ bena: lã, siwo me fli to, nanye wò fetu la, ekema lãha blibo la dzia vi, siwo me fli to la.

ref: That's how God has taken sheep and goats from your father and given them to me.
text: Ale Mawu xɔ lãha blibo le mia fofo si hetsɔ nam.

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.
text: Eye esi lãwo le asi yɔm la, meku drɔ̃e kpɔ bena, lãtsu, siwo me fli to, esiwo le ŋɔtaŋɔta, kple esiwo tso kpɔ sɔŋ, le lãnɔwo liam.

ref: Then God's angel called me by name. I answered,
text: Eye mawudɔla gblɔ nam le drɔ̃e me bena: Yakob! Meɖo ŋu bena: Nye enye si!

ref: and he said, “Notice that all the rams are either spotted or speckled. I know everything Laban is doing to you,
text: Eye wogblɔ bena: Kpɔ ɖa, lãtsu, siwo me fli to, esiwo le ŋɔtaŋɔta, kple esiwo tso kpɔ la, le lãnɔawo liam. Elabena mekpɔ nusi Laban wɔ ɖe ŋuwò la.

ref: and I am the God you worshiped at Bethel, when you poured olive oil on a rock and made a promise to me. Leave here at once and return to the land where you were born.”
text: Nye enye Mawu, si ɖe eɖokui fia wò le Betel, afisi nèkɔ ami ɖe kpe dzi, eye nèɖe adzɔgbe nam le. Tso, naʋu le anyigba sia dzi àgatrɔ yi ɖe mia denyigba dzi!

ref: He treats us like foreigners and has even cheated us out of the bride price that should have been ours.
text: Ɖe mele mia bum amedzrowoe oa? Elabena etsɔ mí dzra, eye wòle ga, si wòxɔ ɖe mía ta la ɖum vaseɖe mi.

ref: So do whatever God tells you to do. Even the property God took from our father and gave to you really belongs to us and our children.
text: Elabena kesinɔnu, siwo katã Mawu xɔ le mía fofo si la, wonye mía ŋutɔwo kple mia viwo tɔ; eyata wɔ nusianu, si Mawu gblɔ ha wò la ko!

ref: Then Jacob, his wives, and his children got on camels and left
text: Tete Yakob tsɔ viawo kple srɔ̃awo ɖo kposɔwo dzi.

ref: northern Syria for the home of his father Isaac in Canaan. Jacob took along all his flocks, herds, and other property.
text: Emegbe la ekplɔ eƒe lãwo katã, eye wotsɔ eƒe kesinɔnuwo katã, nusianu, si wòkpɔ le Mesopotamia la, eye wòdzo yina ɖe fofoa Isak gbɔ le Kanaan-nyigba dzi.

ref: Before Rachel left, she stole the household idols while Laban was out shearing his sheep.
text: Ke esi Laban yi fu sẽ ge na lãwo la, Raxel fi fofoa ƒe aklama kpakpɛwo.

ref: long years to earn your two daughters and another
text: Eye Yakob ble Laban Aramtɔ la, elabena megblɔe nɛ hafi dzo o.

ref: When Jacob crossed the Euphrates River and headed for the hill country of Gilead, he took with him everything he owned. Laban Catches Up with Jacob
text: Ale wòsi yi kple nusiwo katã le esi la, eye wotso tɔsisi la heɖo ta Gilead-towo gbɔ. Yakob wɔ ɖeka kple Laban.

ref: Three days later Laban found out that Jacob had gone.
text: Le ŋkeke etɔ̃ megbe la wova gblɔ na Laban bena, Yakob si.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Tete wotsɔ nɔvia wo kpe ɖe eɖokui ŋu ti eyome ŋkeke adre sɔŋ, eye wòva tui le Gilead-towo dzi.

ref: But God appeared to Laban in a dream that night and warned, “Don't say a word to Jacob. Don't make a threat or a promise.”
text: Eye Mawu va Laban, Aramtɔ la gbɔ le zã me drɔ̃eƒe, eye wògblɔ nɛ bena: Kpɔ ɖokuiwò dzi, be nagaƒo nu yɔa ɖeke kple Yakob o.

ref: Jacob had set up camp in the hill country of Gilead, when Laban and his relatives came and set up camp in another part of the hill country. Laban went to Jacob
text: Eye Laban va tu Yakob; eye Yakob tu eƒe agbadɔ ɖe to la dzi, ke Laban kple nɔviawo tu wo tɔ ɖe Gilead-to la dzi.

ref: and said: Look what you've done! You've tricked me and run off with my daughters like a kidnapper.
text: Tete Laban gblɔ na Yakob bena: Nuka nye esi nèwɔ, eye nèblem hekplɔ vinyenyɔnuwo dzoe abe aboyomewo ene?

ref: Why did you sneak away without telling me? I would have given you a going-away party with singing and with music on tambourines and harps.
text: Nukaŋuti nèsi le adza me, eye nèblem, eye mèklãm, be maɖo wò ɖa kple aseyetsotso, hadzidzi, ʋuƒoƒo kple saŋkuƒoƒo o,

ref: You didn't even give me a chance to kiss my own grandchildren and daughters goodbye. That was really foolish.
text: eye mèna meɖuɖɔ nu na nye tɔgbuiyɔviwo kple vinyenyɔnuwo o mahã? Ewɔ bometsinu!

ref: I could easily hurt you, but the God your father worshiped has warned me not to make any threats or promises.
text: Ŋusẽ le asinye be, mawɔ vɔ̃ mi hafi, gake le etsɔ ƒe zã, si va yi me la, mia fofo ƒe Mawu la gblɔ nam bena: Kpɔ ɖokuiwò dzi, be nagaƒonu vɔ̃a ɖeke kple Yakob o.

ref: I can understand why you were eager to return to your father, but why did you have to steal my idols?
text: Ke esi nèdzo, elabena aƒe le dzrowòm vevie la, nukaŋuti nefi nye mawuwo?

ref: Jacob answered, “I left secretly because I was afraid you would take your daughters from me by force.
text: Tete Yakob ɖo ŋu na Laban bena: Mele vɔvɔ̃m, elabena mesusu be, ɖewohĩ axa viwònyɔnuawo le asinye.

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Amesi ke gbɔ nakpɔ wò mawuawo le la, magano agbe o! Le mía nɔviawo katã ƒe ŋku me ka nye nuwo me, eye natsɔe! Ke Yakob menyae bena. Raxel ye fii o.

ref: Laban searched the tents of Jacob, Leah, and the two servant women, but did not find the idols. Then he went to Rachel's tent.
text: Tete Laban ka Yakob kple Lea kpakple kosi eveawo ƒe agbadɔwo me, mekpɔe o; emegbe la; edze le Lea ƒe agbadɔ me heyi ɖe Raxel ƒe agbadɔ me.

ref: She had already hidden them in the cushion she used as a saddle and was sitting on it. Laban searched everywhere and did not find them.
text: Ke Raxel fi aklama kpakpɛawo, eye wòtsɔ wo ɣla ɖe kposɔ ƒe akpa le hebɔbɔ no edzi. Eye Laban ka agbadɔ la katã me, ke mekpɔ naneke o.

ref: Rachel said, “Father, please don't be angry with me for not getting up; I'm having my period.” Laban kept on searching, but still did not find the idols.
text: Eye wògblɔ na fofoa bena: Nye aƒetɔ, megabi dzi be, nyemate ŋu atsi tre o, elabena mekpɔ dzinu! Eye wódii gadu, ke mekpɔ aklama kpakpɛawo o.

ref: Jacob became very angry and said to Laban: What have I done wrong? Have I committed some crime? Is that why you hunted me down?
text: Azɔ Yakob bi dzi hedo dɔmedzoe ɖe Laban ŋuti. Eye Yakob bia Laban bena. Vo ka meda, eye vɔ ka mewo be, nele hosuhosu dom nam ale?

ref: After searching through everything I have, did you find anything of yours? If so, put it here, where your relatives and mine can see it. Then we can decide what to do.
text: Eka nye nuwo katã me, aƒewo me nu ka nakpɔ mahã? Tsɔe va da ɖe mia kple nɔviwòawo katã ŋku me, bena woatso atia na mia dometɔ, si to dzo!

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: Esiwo nu lé la, nyemetsɔa wo vanɛ na wò o, ke boŋ meɖoa eteƒe na wò, elabena èbianɛ tso asinye, eɖanye wofii le ŋɖɔ alo zã o.

ref: I sweated every day, and I couldn't sleep at night because of the cold.
text: Le ŋkeke me ŋdɔ ɖuam, eye le zã me vuvɔ wɔam, eye nyemedɔa alɔ̃ hã o.

ref: If the fearsome God worshiped by Abraham and my father Isaac had not been on my side, you would have sent me away without a thing. But God saw my hard work, and he knew the trouble I was in, so he helped me. Then last night he told you how wrong you were. Jacob and Laban Make an Agreement
text: Ɖe menye tɔgbuinyewo ƒe Mawu, Abraham ƒe Mawu, si Isak hã vɔ̃na lae, nɔ anyi kplim o la, anye ne ado mɔm asi ƒuƒlui: gake Mawu kpo nye fukpekpe kple nye agbagbadzedze la, eyata wòtso afia nam etso ƒe zã, si va yi.

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “Leah and Rachel are my daughters, and their children belong to me. All these sheep you are taking are really mine too. In fact, everything you have belongs to me. But there is nothing I can do to keep my daughters and their children.
text: Tete Laban ɖo ŋu na Yakob bena: Nyɔnuawo tɔnyewoe, ɖevia wo hã tɔnyewoe, lãwo tɔnyewoe, eye nusianu, si kpam nele la, tɔnyee! Ke nuka mawɔ ɖe vinyenyɔnuwo kple vi, siwo wodzi la ŋu?

ref: So I am ready to make an agreement with you, and we will pile up some large rocks here to remind us of the agreement.”
text: Na míabla nu kple mía nɔewo, eye esia anye ɖase le mia kpli wò dome.

ref: After Jacob had set up a large rock,
text: Tete Yakob tso kpea ɖe tu anyi, be wòanye dzesikpe.

ref: he told his men to get some more rocks and pile them up next to it. Then Jacob and Laban ate a meal together beside the rocks.
text: Eye Yakob gblɔ na nɔviawo bena: Mifa kpewo vɛ! Eye wofɔ kpewo li koe; emegbe la woɖu nu le afima le ka la dzi.

ref: Laban named the pile of rocks Jegar Sahadutha. But Jacob named it Galeed.
text: Eye Laban na ŋkɔe be Yegar-Saxaduta; ke Yakob na ŋkɔe bena Galed.

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “This pile of rocks will remind us of our agreement.” That's why the place was named Galeed.
text: Eye Laban gblɔ bena: Kpekɔ sia enye ɖase le mia kple wò dome egbe. Eyata wona ŋkɔe bena Galed kple Mizpa,

ref: Laban also said, “This pile of rocks means that the Lord will watch us both while we are apart from each other.” So the place was also named Mizpah.
text: elabena egblɔ be Yehowa anɔ mia kple wò ŋuti dzom, ne miegale adzɔge tso mia nɔewo gbɔ hã la.

ref: Then Laban said: If you mistreat my daughters or marry other women, I may not know about it, but remember, God is watching us!
text: Ne ate vinyenyɔnuwo ɖe anyi, alo naɖe srɔ̃ bubu akpe ɖe vinyenyɔnuawo ŋu la — amea ɖeke mele mia gbɔ o, Mawu koe nye ɖaseɖila le mia kpli wo dome.

ref: Both this pile of rocks and this large rock have been set up between us as a reminder. I must never go past them to attack you, and you must never come past them to attack me.
text: Kpekɔ sia kple dzesikpe la anye ɖase Nyemekpɔ mo ato kpekɔ sia kple dzesikpe sia ŋu ava gbɔwo o, eye wò hã mekpe mo aɖo tame vɔ̃ ato kpekɔ sia kple dzesikpe sia ŋu ava gbɔnye o!

ref: My father Nahor, your grandfather Abraham, and their ancestors all worshiped the same God, and he will make sure that we each keep the agreement. Then Jacob made a promise in the name of the fearsome God his father Isaac had worshiped.
text: Abraham ƒe Mawu, Naxor ƒe Mawu kple wo tɔgbuiwo ƒe Mawu la anye ʋɔnudrɔ̃la le mía kpli wò dome. Eye Yakob tsɔ amesi fofoa Isak vɔ̃na la, ka atam.

ref: Jacob killed an animal and offered it as a sacrifice there on the mountain, and he invited his men to eat with him. After the meal they spent the night on the mountain.
text: Emegbe la Yakob wu vɔsalãwo le to la dzi, eye wokpe nɔviawo be, woava ɖu nu. Eye woɖu nu, eye wotsi to la dzi dɔ.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Eye esi Laban fo ŋdi kanya la, eɖuɖɔ nu na tɔgbuiayɔviwo kple vianyɔnuwo, eye wòyra wo. Emegbe la Laban dze mɔ gatrɔ yi enɔƒe.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Ke Yakob hã dze eƒe ma dzi, eye mawudɔlawo va do goe.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: Eye esi Yakob kpɔ wo la, egblɔ be Mawu ƒe asaɖa enye esia! Eyata wòyɔ teƒe ma be Maxanaim. Yakob vi Esau.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: Eye Yakob dɔ dɔlawo ɖo ɖe eŋgɔ ɖe nɔvia Esau gbɔ le Seir-nyigba dzi le Edom gbedzi.

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”
text: Eye wògblɔ na wo bena: Ale miagblɔ na nye aƒetɔ Esau enye si: Wò dɔla Yakob be, míagblɔ na wò bena: Menɔ Laban gbɔ, eye metsi anyi ʋuu vaseɖe fifi.

ref: When the messengers returned, they told Jacob, “We went to your brother Esau, and now he is heading this way with
text: Eye mekpɔ aƒenyiwo, tedziwo kple alẽwo, kluviwo kple kosiviwo, eye meɖo dɔlawo ɖo ɖe nye aƒetɔ gbɔ, be wòave nunye.

ref: Jacob was so frightened that he divided his people, sheep, cattle, and camels into two groups.
text: Eye dɔlawo gatrɔ va Yakob gbɔ, eye wogblɔ bena: Miede noviwò Esau gbɔ, eye eya hã gbɔna kpe wò ge kple ame alafa ene.

ref: He thought, “If Esau attacks one group, perhaps the other can escape.”
text: Tete Yakob de asi vɔvɔ̃ me ŋutɔ, eye dzika tso eƒo, eye wòmã amesiwo le eŋu, alẽwo, aƒenyiwo kple kposɔwo ɖe akpa eve me.

ref: Then Jacob prayed: You, Lord, are the God who was worshiped by my grandfather Abraham and by my father Isaac. You told me to return home to my family, and you promised to be with me and make me successful.
text: Elabena egblɔ bena: Ne Esau ava dze akpa ɖeka dzi, eye woade zi wo me la, ekema akpa ɖeka nasi adzo. Yakob ƒe gbedodoɖa.

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: nye medze wò nublanui, si nèkpɔ nam, kple wò anukware, si nẽɖi na wò dɔla la dometɔa ɖeke o; elabena nye ati sia metsɔ tso Yordan sia, ke azɔ la metrɔ zu asafokɔ eve.

ref: But you have promised that I would be a success and that someday it will be as hard to count my descendants as it is to count the grains of sand along the seashore.
text: Meɖe kuku, ɖem tso nɔvinye si me, tso Esau si me; elabena mele evɔ̃m be, ava dze dzinye, eye wòaɖe zi dadawo kple viwo dome.

ref: And Jacob told them to be sure to say that he was right behind them. Jacob hoped the gifts would make Esau friendly, so Esau would be glad to see him when they met.
text: Nenema wògblɔ na ame evelia, etɔ̃lia kple mamle, siwo katã le lãhawo kplɔm la, eye wògblɔ bena: Alea tututu miagblɔ na Esau, ne mieɖo goe!

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Tete Yakob biae bena: Meɖe kuku, yɔ wò ŋkɔ nam! Eɖo ŋu bena: Nukaŋuti gɔ̃ nèle nye ŋkɔ ta biam? Eye wòyrae lé afima.

ref: Jacob put servants in charge of each herd and told them, “Go ahead of me and keep a space between each herd.”
text: kposɔ vinɔ blaetɔ̃ kple wo viwo, nyinɔ blane kple nyitsu ewo, tedzinɔ blave kple tedzivi ewo.

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Eye wogblɔna ŋgɔgbetɔ bena: Ne nɔvinye ava do go wò, eye woabia wò bena: Ameka vi nènye, afika nèyina, eye nusiwo le ŋgɔwò la, ameka tɔe mahã la,

ref: Jacob also told the men in charge of the second and third herds and those who followed to say the same thing when they met Esau.
text: ekema naɖo ŋu bena. Wò dɔla Yakob ɖo nu siawo ɖe nye aƒetɔ Esau: eye eya ŋutɔ le mia megbe gbɔna.

ref: Jacob's men took the gifts on ahead of him, but he spent the night in camp. Jacob's Name Is Changed to Israel
text: Eye miagblɔ bena: Wò dɔla Yakob hã le mía megbe gbɔna. Elabena egblɔ be, yeatsɔ nunana, siwo dze ŋgɔ nɛ la, akpatae gbã, hafi ye ŋutɔ yeado ɖe eƒe ŋku me, ɖewo ave ye nu axɔ ye matu.

ref: Jacob got up in the middle of the night and took his wives, his eleven children, and everything he owned across to the other side of the Jabbok River for safety.
text: Emegbe la efɔ le zã ma me, eye eya kple esrɔ̃ eveawo kple kosi eveawo kpe ɖe ɖevi wuiɖekɛawo ŋu, wotso Yabok tɔsisi la.

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: Eye esi wòna woɖe tsi wo kple nusiwo katã le esi, yi ɖe tɔa godo la,

ref: When the man saw that he could not win, he struck Jacob on the hip and threw it out of joint.
text: Yakob ɖeɖe tsi megbe. Tete ŋutsua ɖe va te kame kplii ʋuu, vaseɖe esime agu dze.

ref: They kept on wrestling until the man said, “Let go of me! It's almost daylight.” “You can't go until you bless me,” Jacob replied.
text: Ke esi wòkpɔ be, yemate ŋui o la, etɔ asi eƒe aligo, eye Yakob ƒe aligokpeƒe ɖɔ li, le esime wole ka mea tem la.

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Eye wògblɔ bena: Womagayɔ wò be Yakob akpɔ o, ke boŋ Israel, elabena ete kame kple Mawu kpakple ame, eye nete ŋui.

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Eye Yakob na ŋkɔ teƒe la be Pniel, elabena egblɔ be: Mekpɔ Mawu ŋku me kple ŋku me: gake metsi agbe.

ref: and the muscle on his hip joint had been injured. That's why even today the people of Israel don't eat the hip muscle of any animal.
text: Ke esi wòɖe Pniel ɖe megbe la, ɣe dze, eye wòle tɔtɔm ɖe aku dzi.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Eye esi Laban fo ŋdi kanya la, eɖuɖɔ nu na tɔgbuiayɔviwo kple vianyɔnuwo, eye wòyra wo. Emegbe la Laban dze mɔ gatrɔ yi enɔƒe.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Ke Yakob hã dze eƒe ma dzi, eye mawudɔlawo va do goe.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: Eye esi Yakob kpɔ wo la, egblɔ be Mawu ƒe asaɖa enye esia! Eyata wòyɔ teƒe ma be Maxanaim. Yakob vi Esau.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: Eye Yakob dɔ dɔlawo ɖo ɖe eŋgɔ ɖe nɔvia Esau gbɔ le Seir-nyigba dzi le Edom gbedzi.

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”
text: Eye wògblɔ na wo bena: Ale miagblɔ na nye aƒetɔ Esau enye si: Wò dɔla Yakob be, míagblɔ na wò bena: Menɔ Laban gbɔ, eye metsi anyi ʋuu vaseɖe fifi.

ref: When the messengers returned, they told Jacob, “We went to your brother Esau, and now he is heading this way with
text: Eye mekpɔ aƒenyiwo, tedziwo kple alẽwo, kluviwo kple kosiviwo, eye meɖo dɔlawo ɖo ɖe nye aƒetɔ gbɔ, be wòave nunye.

ref: Jacob was so frightened that he divided his people, sheep, cattle, and camels into two groups.
text: Eye dɔlawo gatrɔ va Yakob gbɔ, eye wogblɔ bena: Miede noviwò Esau gbɔ, eye eya hã gbɔna kpe wò ge kple ame alafa ene.

ref: He thought, “If Esau attacks one group, perhaps the other can escape.”
text: Tete Yakob de asi vɔvɔ̃ me ŋutɔ, eye dzika tso eƒo, eye wòmã amesiwo le eŋu, alẽwo, aƒenyiwo kple kposɔwo ɖe akpa eve me.

ref: Then Jacob prayed: You, Lord, are the God who was worshiped by my grandfather Abraham and by my father Isaac. You told me to return home to my family, and you promised to be with me and make me successful.
text: Elabena egblɔ bena: Ne Esau ava dze akpa ɖeka dzi, eye woade zi wo me la, ekema akpa ɖeka nasi adzo. Yakob ƒe gbedodoɖa.

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: nye medze wò nublanui, si nèkpɔ nam, kple wò anukware, si nẽɖi na wò dɔla la dometɔa ɖeke o; elabena nye ati sia metsɔ tso Yordan sia, ke azɔ la metrɔ zu asafokɔ eve.

ref: But you have promised that I would be a success and that someday it will be as hard to count my descendants as it is to count the grains of sand along the seashore.
text: Meɖe kuku, ɖem tso nɔvinye si me, tso Esau si me; elabena mele evɔ̃m be, ava dze dzinye, eye wòaɖe zi dadawo kple viwo dome.

ref: And Jacob told them to be sure to say that he was right behind them. Jacob hoped the gifts would make Esau friendly, so Esau would be glad to see him when they met.
text: Nenema wògblɔ na ame evelia, etɔ̃lia kple mamle, siwo katã le lãhawo kplɔm la, eye wògblɔ bena: Alea tututu miagblɔ na Esau, ne mieɖo goe!

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Tete Yakob biae bena: Meɖe kuku, yɔ wò ŋkɔ nam! Eɖo ŋu bena: Nukaŋuti gɔ̃ nèle nye ŋkɔ ta biam? Eye wòyrae lé afima.

ref: Jacob put servants in charge of each herd and told them, “Go ahead of me and keep a space between each herd.”
text: kposɔ vinɔ blaetɔ̃ kple wo viwo, nyinɔ blane kple nyitsu ewo, tedzinɔ blave kple tedzivi ewo.

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: Eye wogblɔna ŋgɔgbetɔ bena: Ne nɔvinye ava do go wò, eye woabia wò bena: Ameka vi nènye, afika nèyina, eye nusiwo le ŋgɔwò la, ameka tɔe mahã la,

ref: Jacob also told the men in charge of the second and third herds and those who followed to say the same thing when they met Esau.

ref: The two servant women, Zilpah and Bilhah, together with their children went first, followed by Leah and her children, then by Rachel and Joseph.
text: Eye wòtsɔ kosi eveawo kple wo viwo ɖo ŋgɔ, eyome Lea kple viawo, emegbe Raxel kple Yosef.

ref: Jacob himself walked in front of them all, bowing to the ground seven times as he came near his brother.
text: Ke eya ŋuta dze wo ŋgɔ, eye wotsyɔ mo anyi zi adre, vaseɖe esime wòle tu nɔvia.

ref: But Esau ran toward Jacob and hugged and kissed him. Then the two brothers started crying.
text: Tete Esau ƒu du va kpee, kpla asi kɔ nɛ, eye wòɖuɖɔ nu nɛ, eye wòde asi avifafa me.

ref: When Esau noticed the women and children he asked, “Whose children are these?” Jacob answered, “These are the children the Lord has been kind enough to give to me, your servant.”
text: Eye esi wòfɔ kɔ dzi kpɔ nyɔnuawo kple ɖeviawo la, ebia bena: Amekawo nye esiawo le ŋuwò mahã? Eɖo ŋu bena: Woawoe nye vi, siwo Mawu na wò dɔla!

ref: Then the two servant women and their children came and bowed down to Esau.
text: Tete kosiawo kple wo viwo va dze klo,

ref: Next, Leah and her children came and bowed down; finally, Joseph and Rachel also came and bowed down.
text: eyome Lea kple viawo va dze klo, emegbe Yosef kple Raxel wova dze klo.

ref: Esau asked Jacob, “Why did you send those herds I met along the road?” “Master,” Jacob answered, “I sent them so you would be friendly to me.”
text: Eye wòbiɛ bena: Ha blibo ka enye si va kpem le mɔ dzi? Eɖo ŋu be: Bena nye aƒetɔ nave nunye!

ref: “But, brother, I already have plenty,” Esau replied. “Keep them for yourself.”
text: Esau gblɔ bena: Nu geɖe le asinye, nɔvinye, wò nuwo nenɔ asiwò ko.

ref: “No!” Jacob said. “Please accept them as a sign of your friendship for me. When you welcomed me and I saw your face, it was like seeing the face of God.
text: Yakob ɖo ŋu nɛ bena: Gbeɖe! Ne ave nunye la, ekema xɔ nye nunana la! Elabena mekpɔ wò ŋku me abe Mawu ƒe ŋku mee mekpɔ ene, eye nève nunye.

ref: Please accept these as gifts I brought to you. God has been good to me, and I have everything I need.” Jacob kept insisting until Esau agreed.
text: Xa nye nusi wotsɔ vɛ na wò la dzro, elabena Yehowa yram, mekpɔ nu geɖe! Eye wótèe ɖe ŋu, vaseɖe esime wòxɔe.

ref: “Let's get ready to travel,” Esau said. “I'll go along with you.”
text: Eye wògblɔ bena: Na miadze mɔ, ke nye la madze ŋgɔwò.

ref: But Jacob answered, “Master, you know traveling is hard on children, and I have to look after the sheep and goats that are nursing their young. If my animals travel too much in one day, they will all die.
text: Eɖo ŋu ne bena: Nye aƒetɔ, wò ŋutɔ ènyae bena, ɖevi dzawo kple alẽ kple nyi vinɔwo le ŋunye ne woaƒo du ɖe wo nu ŋkeke ɖeka ko la, ekema lãha blibo la atsrɔ̃.

ref: Why don't you go on ahead and let me travel along slowly with the children, the herds, and the flocks. We can meet again in the country of Edom.”
text: Meɖe kuku, nye aƒetɔ nedze eƒe dɔla ŋgɔ, eye nye la madze yowòme blewublewu, abe alesi lãha la kple ɖevi dzawo ate ŋui ene, vaseɖe esime matu nye aƒetɔ le Seir.

ref: Esau replied, “Let me leave some of my men with you.” “You don't have to do that,” Jacob answered. “I am happy, simply knowing that you are friendly to me.”
text: Tete Esau gblɔ bena: Ekema magblẽ amesiwo le ŋunye la ɖewo ɖe gbɔwò! Eɖo ŋu bena: Nukaŋuti, esi nye aƒetɔ ve nunye xoxo ɖe!

ref: But Jacob went to Succoth, where he built a house for himself and set up shelters for his animals. That's why the place is called Succoth. Jacob Arrives at Shechem
text: Eye Yakob dze mɔ va Sukot, eye wòtu xɔ ɖe afima, ke ewɔ agbadɔ na eƒe lãhawo; eyata woyɔ afima bena Sukot.

ref: After leaving northern Syria, Jacob arrived safely at Shechem in Canaan and set up camp outside the city.
text: Esi Yakob ʋu tso Mesopotamia la, eɖo Sixem, du, si le Kanaan-nyigba dzi la me dedie, eye wònɔ dua xa.

ref: The land where he camped was owned by the descendants of Hamor, the father of Shechem. So Jacob paid them
text: Eye wòƒle anyigba, si dzi wòtu eƒe agbadɔ ɖo la, le Sixem fofo Hemor ƒe viwo si, kesita alafa ɖeka.

ref: then he set up his tents and built an altar there to honor the God of Israel.
text: Eye wòɖi vɔsamlekpui ete afima, eye wòna ŋkɔe bena: „Mawu, si enye Israel ƒe Mawu“.

ref: Dinah, the daughter of Jacob and Leah, went to visit some of the women who lived nearby.
text: Gbe ɖeka Dina, Lea ƒe vinyɔnu, tsi wòdzi na Yakob la, do go yi dua me nyɔnuviwo kpɔ ge ɖa.

ref: She was seen by Hamor's son Shechem, the leader of the Hivites, and he grabbed her and raped her.
text: Tete anyigba ma dzi fia, Xamor Xiwitɔ, ƒe ŋutsuvi Sixem kpɔe, eye wolee de asi eŋu, hegblẽe sesẽe.

ref: But Shechem was attracted to Dinah, so he told her how much he loved her.
text: Eye eƒe dzi ku de Yakob ƒe vinyɔnu Dina ŋu, eye wolã ɖetugbivi la, eye wòde asi eŋu beble me.

ref: Shechem even asked his father to arrange for him to marry her.
text: Emegbe la Sixem gblɔ na fofoa Xamor bena: Ɖe ɖetugbi sia nam!

ref: Meanwhile, Jacob heard what had happened. But his sons were out in the fields with the cattle, so he did not do anything at the time.
text: Eye Yakob se be, wogblẽ vianyɔnu Dina, ke viaŋutsuwo le gbedzi le eƒe alẽhawo gbɔ, eye Yakob zi kpi, vaseɖe esime wogbɔ.

ref: just as Jacob's sons were coming in from work. When they learned that their sister had been raped, they became furiously angry, because nothing is more disgraceful than rape, and it must not be tolerated.
text: Esi Yakob viŋutsuwo gbɔ tso lãnyiƒe, eye wosee la, ŋutsuawo bi dzi, eye wodo dɔmedzoe ŋutɔ; elabena ewɔ ŋukpenanu ɖe Israel ŋuti, be wòde asi Yakob ƒe vinyɔnu ŋuti, nusi medze kura o.

ref: Hamor said to Jacob and his sons: My son Shechem really loves Dinah. Please let him marry her.
text: Eye Xamor ƒo nu kpli wo gblɔ bena: Vinye Sixem ƒe dzi ku de mia nɔvinyɔnu la ŋuti; mede kuku, mitsɔe nɛ, ne wòaɖe.

ref: Why don't you start letting your families marry into our families and ours marry into yours?
text: Eye mina miaɖe srɔ̃ ɖekae, mitsɔ miaƒe nyɔnuviwo na mí, eye miawo hã miatsɔ míaƒe nyɔnuviwo na mi.

ref: You can share this land with us. Move freely about until you find the property you want; then buy it and settle down here.
text: Minɔ mía gbɔ, kpɔ ɖa, anyigba la enye si, minɔ edzi, eye miatsa le edzi, eye míatsi edzi dika.

ref: Shechem added, “Do this favor for me, and I'll give whatever you want.
text: Tete Sixem gblɔ na fofoa kple nɔviawo bena: Ne miave nunye la, ekema nusianu, si miabiam la, mana.

ref: Ask anything, no matter how expensive. I'll do anything, just let me marry Dinah.”
text: Tabianu kple akpedanu, alesi nu miabiam la, mana, abe alesi miedi ene; mitsɔ ɖetugbivi la nam made ko.

ref: Jacob's sons wanted to get even with Shechem and his father because of what had happened to their sister.
text: Eye Yakob viŋutsuwo do ŋu na Sixem kple fofoa Xamor le aye me, elabena egblẽ wo nɔvinyɔnu Dina.

ref: So they tricked them by saying: You're not circumcised! It would be a disgrace for us to let you marry Dinah now.
text: Eye wogblɔ na wo bena: Míate ŋu awɔ nya sia dzi be, miatsɔ mía nɔvinyɔnu na bolobolotɔ o, elabena esia enye ŋukpe na mí.

ref: But we will let you marry her, if you and the other men in your tribe agree to be circumcised.
text: Negbe ne mianɔ abe miawo ene, eye mia dome ŋutsu sia ŋutsu natso aʋa la, eya ko hafi míalɔ̃.

ref: Then your families can marry into ours, and ours can marry into yours, and we can live together like one nation.
text: Ekema miatsɔ mía nyɔnuviwo na mi, eye miawo hã miaɖe mia nyɔnuviwo; mianɔ mia gbɔ, eye míazu dukɔ ɖeka.

ref: But if you don't agree to be circumcised, we'll take Dinah and leave this place.
text: Ke ne mieɖo to mi, eye mietso aʋa o la, ekema miaxɔ mía nɔvinyɔnu la adzoe.

ref: Hamor and Shechem liked what was said.
text: Nya siawo dze to na Xamor kple via Sixem.

ref: Shechem was the most respected person in his family, and he was so in love with Dinah that he hurried off to get everything done.
text: Eye ɖekakpui la, amesi fofoa ƒemetɔwo buna ŋutɔ la, meɖe: ɖe megbe le ewɔwɔ me o, elabena Yakob vinyɔnu la ƒe nu nyo eŋu ŋuta.

ref: The two men met with the other leaders of their city and told them:
text: Eye Xamor kple via Sixem wova du la ƒe agbo nu, eye woƒo nu kple dua me ŋutsuwo bena:

ref: These people really are friendly. Why not let them move freely about until they find the property they want? There's enough land here for them and for us. Then our families can marry into theirs, and theirs can marry into ours.
text: Ŋutsu siawo nye ame fafawo le mia dome; eyata mina, woatso aƒe ɖe miaƒe anyigba dzi, eye woatsa le edzi. Kpɔ ɖa, anyigba la keke na wo le goawo katã me. Miaɖe woƒe nyonuviwo faa, eye miawo hã miatsɔ mía nyɔnuviwo na wo.

ref: We have to do only one thing before they will agree to stay here and become one nation with us. Our men will have to be circumcised just like theirs.
text: Ne mía dome ŋutsu sia ŋutsu atso aʋa, abe alesi woawo hã tso aʋae ene la, eya ko ŋutsuawo alɔ̃ anɔ mía gbɔ, bena míazu dukɔ ɖeka.

ref: Just think! We'll get their property, as well as their flocks and herds. All we have to do is to agree, and they will live here with us.
text: Woƒe lãhawo, kesinɔnuwo kple woƒe dɔwɔlãwo katã la menye mia tɔ, woanye oa? Eyata mina míawɔ woƒe nya la dzi, ekema woanɔ mia gbɔ.

ref: Three days later the men who had been circumcised were still weak from pain. So Simeon and Levi, two of Dinah's brothers, attacked with their swords and killed every man in the town,
text: Le ŋkeke etɔ̃agbe, esi abi la le wo vem, eye woɖe ŋu ɖi la, Yakob ƒe viŋutsu eve, Simeon kple Lewi, siwo nye Dina nɔviwò lã, wotsɔ woƒe yiwo dze du la dzi hewu ŋutsuwo katã.

ref: including Hamor and Shechem. Then they took Dinah and left.
text: Wowu Xamor kple via Sixem hã kple yi, eye wokplɔ Dina tso Sixem ƒe me hedzoe.

ref: Jacob's other sons came and took everything they wanted. All this was done because of the horrible thing that had happened to their sister.
text: Ke Yakob vi mamleawo va ɖe nu le ame kukuawo ŋu, eye woha du la, elabena wogblẽ wo nɔvinyɔnu.

ref: They took sheep, goats, donkeys, and everything else that was in the town or the countryside.
text: Eye wokplɔ woƒe alẽwo, nyiwo kple tedziwo, eve wotso nusiwo wokpɔ le dua me kple agble me la, hedzoe

ref: After taking everything of value from the houses, they dragged away the wives and children of their victims.
text: Woha woƒe kesinɔnuwo katã, eye woɖe aboyo ɖeviwo kple nyɔnuwo siaa, eye woha woƒe xɔme nuwo hã.

ref: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “Look what you've done! Now I'm in real trouble with the Canaanites and Perizzites who live around here. There aren't many of us, and if they attack, they'll kill everyone in my household.”
text: Tete Yakob gblɔ na Simeon kple Lewi bena: Miehe dzɔgbevɔ̃e va dzinye, eye anyi gbadzinɔla, siwo nye Kanaanitɔwo kple Perizitɔwo la, woalem ɖe fu me: kpɔ ɖa, ame ʋeea ɖewo koe míenye, eye ne woaƒo ƒu ɖe mia ŋu la, ekema woaɖu mía dzi, eye woatsrɔ̃m kple tɔnyetɔwo katã.

ref: They answered, “Was it right to let our own sister be treated that way?”
text: Woɖo ŋu ne bena: Ɖe eya kpɔ mɔ abu mia nɔvi gboloe mahã?

ref: God told Jacob, “Return to Bethel, where I appeared to you when you were running from your brother Esau. Make your home there and build an altar for me.”
text: Eye Mawu gblɔ na Yakob bena: Tso, naʋu ayi Betel, eye nanɔ afima, eye naɖi vɔsamlekpui ɖe afima na Mawu, si ɖe eɖokui fia wò, esime nesi le nɔviwò Esau nu!

ref: Jacob said to his family and to everyone else who was traveling with him: Get rid of your foreign gods! Then make yourselves acceptable to worship God and put on clean clothes.
text: Tete Yakob gblɔ na eƒemetɔwo kple amesiwo katã le egbɔ la, bena: Miɖe dzromawu, siwo le mia dome la da, miklɔ mia ɖokui ŋuti, eye miɖɔ li miaƒe awuwo,

ref: Afterwards, we'll go to Bethel. I will build an altar there for God, who answered my prayers when I was in trouble and who has always been at my side.
text: eye míaʋu ayi Betel, bena maɖi vɔsamlekpui ɖe afima na Mawu, si ɖo tom, esi mele xaxa me, eye wònɔ anyi kplim le nye mɔzɔzɔ me la!

ref: So everyone gave Jacob their idols and their earrings, and he buried them under the oak tree near Shechem.
text: Tete wotsɔ dzromawu, siwo katã le wo si, kple woƒe togɛwo na Yakob, eye Yakob ɖi wo ɖe ati gã, si le Sixem gbɔ la te.

ref: While Jacob and his family were traveling through Canaan, God terrified the people in the towns so much that no one dared bother them.
text: Emegbe la wodze mɔ, ke Mawu na vɔvɔ̃ va dze du, siwo ƒo xlã wo la dzi: eyata wometi Yakob viwo yome o.

ref: Finally, they reached Bethel, also known as Luz.
text: Eye Yakob kple amesiwo katã le eŋu la, wova Lus, esia nye Betel, le Kanaan-nyigba dzi.

ref: Jacob built an altar there and called it “God of Bethel,” because that was the place where God had appeared to him when he was running from Esau.
text: Eye wòɖi vɔsamlekpui ɖe afima, eye wòyɔ teƒe la be „Betel ƒe Mawu“, elabena afima Mawu ɖe eɖokui fiae le, esime wòsi le nɔvia nu.

ref: While they were there, Rebekah's personal servant Deborah died. They buried her under an oak tree and called it “Weeping Oak.” God Blesses Jacob at Bethel
text: Ke Debora, amesi na no Rebeka le ɖevime la ku, eye woɖii ɖe Betel ƒe anyigbe ɖe logoti gãa ɖe te, eyata wona ŋkɔ afima bena „Konyifalogo“. Mawu na ŋkɔ yeye Yakob, eye wòyrae.

ref: I will give you the land that I promised Abraham and Isaac, and it will belong to your family forever.
text: Eye anyigba, si metsɔ na Abraham kple Isak la, màtsɔe ana wò, eye matsɔ anyigba la ana megbewòviwo.

ref: Jacob set up a large rock, so that he would remember what had happened there. Then he poured wine and olive oil on the rock to show that it was dedicated to God,
text: Tete Yakob tu dzesikpe, si enye ŋkuɖodzikpea ɖe, ɖe afisi wòƒo nu kplii le la, eye wòkɔ aha ɖe edzi, emegbe la ekɔ ami hã ɖe edzi.

ref: and he named the place Bethel. Benjamin Is Born
text: Eye Yakob na ŋkɔ teƒe, si Mawu ƒo nu kplii le la, bena Betel. Raxel ƒe ku.

ref: Jacob and his family had left Bethel and were still a long way from Ephrath, when the time came for Rachel's baby to be born.
text: Emegbe la wodze mɔ tso Betel. Esi wote lu Efrat la, Raxel de asi kulele me: ke vidzidzi la nye dɔ nɛ.

ref: She was having a rough time, but the woman who was helping her said, “Don't worry! It's a boy.”
text: Eye esi vidzidzia nye dɔ nɛ la, vixela la gblɔ nɛ bena: Megavɔ̃ o, elabena esia hã nye ŋutsuvi!

ref: Rachel was at the point of death, and right before dying, she said, “I'll name him Benoni.” But Jacob called him Benjamin.
text: Esi wòle kudɔ ƒom, eye wòkpɔ bena, yele kuku ge la, ena ŋkɔe bena: Benoni; ke fofoa yɔe be Benyamin.

ref: Rachel was buried beside the road to Ephrath, which is also called Bethlehem.
text: Emegbe la Raxel ku, eye woɖu ɖe Efrat, si enye Betlexem, mɔ to.

ref: Jacob set up a tombstone over her grave, and it is still there.
text: Eye Yakob tu dzesikpe ɖe eƒe yɔdo dzi, esia nye ŋkuɖodzikpe le Raxel ƒe yɔdo dzi vaseɖe egbegbe.

ref: Jacob, also known as Israel, traveled to the south of Eder Tower, where he set up camp.
text: Emegbe la Israel gadze mɔ, eye wòtu eƒe agbadɔ ɖe Migdal-Edel godo. Yakob viŋutsuwo.

ref: During their time there, Jacob's oldest son Reuben slept with Bilhah, who was one of Jacob's other wives. And Jacob found out about it. Jacob's Twelve Sons
text: Ke esi Israel le nuto sia me la, Ruben de asi fofoa ƒe kosi Bilha ŋu, eye Israel see.

ref: Jacob went to his father Isaac at Hebron, also called Mamre or Kiriath-Arba, where Isaac's father Abraham had lived as a foreigner.
text: Eye Yakob va fofoa Isak gbɔ le Mamre le Kiryat-Arba, esia nye Xebron, afisi Abraham kple Isak wonye amedzrowo le.

ref: Esau, also known as Edom, had many descendants.
text: Esia enye Esau, si enye Edom, ƒe dzidzimeviwo ŋutinya.

ref: He married three Canaanite women: The first was Adah, the daughter of Elon the Hittite; the second was Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah and the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
text: Esau ɖe Kanaan-nyɔnuwo, woawo nye Ada, Xititɔ Elon ƒe vinyɔnu, Oholibama, Ana ƒe vinyɔnu, amesi enye Xititɔ Zibeon ƒe tɔgbuiyɔvi;

ref: the third was Basemath, who was Ishmael's daughter and Nebaioth's sister.
text: kple Basmat, Ismael ƒe vinyɔnu, si enye Nebayot ƒe nɔvinyɔnu.

ref: Esau and his three wives had five sons while in Canaan. Adah's son was Eliphaz; Basemath's son was Reuel; Oholibamah's three sons were Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
text: Eye Oholibama dzi Yehus, Yaelem kple Korax. Esiawo nye viŋutsu, siwo wodzi na Esau le Kanaan-nyigba dzi.

ref: Esau took his children and wives, his relatives and servants, his animals and possessions he had acquired while in Canaan, and moved far from Jacob.
text: Eye Esau kplu srɔ̃awo, viaŋutsuwo kple vianyɔnuwo kple eƒe kluvi, siwo katã le eƒe me, kple eƒe lãhawo kple eƒe dɔwɔlãwo katã kpe ɖe eƒe kesinɔnu, siwo katã wòkpɔ le Kanaan-nyigba dzi la ŋuti, eye woʋu le nɔvia Yakob gbɔ heyi ɖe Seir-nyigba dzi.

ref: He did this because the land was too crowded and could not support him and his brother with their flocks and herds.
text: Elabena woƒe kesinɔnuwo so gbɔ akpa, ale bena womate ŋu anɔ wo nɔewo gbɔ o; eye anyigba, si dzi wonye amedzro le la, mede wo nu o, le woƒe lãhawo ta.

ref: That's why Esau made his home in the hill country of Seir.
text: Eye Esau nɔ Seir-to la dzi. Esau enye Edom.

ref: Esau and Adah's oldest son was Eliphaz, and the clans that descended from him were Teman, Omar, Zepho, Kenaz,
text: Ame siawo nye amegãwo le Esau viwo dome. Esau ƒe ŋgɔgbevi Elifaz viwo nye, amegã Teman, amegã Omar, amegã Zefo, amegã Kenas,

ref: Korah, Gatam, and Amalek. These and Esau's other descendants lived in the land of Edom.
text: amegã Korax, amegã Gaetan kple amegã Amalek. Amegã siawo dzɔ tso Elifaz me le Edom-nyigba dzi. Woawo nye Ada viwo.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah were Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
text: Esiawo nye Esau srɔ̃ Oholibama ƒe viwo. Amegã Yehus, amegã Yaelam kple amegã Korax. Amegã siawo dzo tso Esau srɔ̃ Oholibama, si enye Ana ƒe vinyɔnu la me.

ref: All of these clans descended from Esau, who was known as Edom.
text: Esiawo nye Esau ƒe viŋutsuwo, eye woawo nye woƒe amegãwo Eya nye Edom.

ref: Seir was from the Horite tribe that had lived in Edom before the time of Esau. The clans that had descended from him were Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
text: Esiawo nye Xoritɔ Seir, amesi nɔ anyigba la dzi tsã la, ƒe viŋutsuwo Lotan, Sobal, Zibeon, Ana,

ref: Lotan's sons were Hori and Heman; his sister was Timna.
text: Lotan ƒe viŋutsuwo nye: Xori kple Hemam; eye Lotan nɔvinyɔnu enye Timna.

ref: Shobal's sons were Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
text: Esiawo nye Sobal ƒe viŋutsuwo: Alwan, Manaxat, Ebal, Sefo kple Onam.

ref: Zibeon's sons were Aiah and Anah—the same Anah who found an oasis in the desert while taking the donkeys of his father out to pasture.
text: Esiawo nye Zibeon ƒe viŋutsuwo: Aya kple Ana; eya nye Ana ma, si tɔ tsi dzodzoe vudo le gbedzi, esime wòle fofoa Zibeon ƒe tedziwo kplɔm la.

ref: Anah's children were Dishon and Oholibamah.
text: Esiawo nye Ana ƒe viwo: Dison kple Ana ƒe vinyɔnu Oholibama.

ref: Dishon's sons were Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran.
text: Esiawo nye Dison ƒe viŋutsuwo: Xemdan, Esban, Yitran kple Keran.

ref: Ezer's sons were Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan.
text: Ezer viŋutsuwo nye Bilhan, Sawan kple Akan.

ref: Dishan's sons were Uz and Aran.
text: Disan viŋutsuwo nye: Uz kple Aran.

ref: The clans of the Horites were Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah,
text: Esiawo nye Xoritɔwo ƒe amegãwo: Amegã Lotan, amegã Sobal, amegã Zibeon, amegã Ana,

ref: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan, and they lived in the land of Seir.
text: amegã Dison, amegã Ezer kple amegã Disan. Esiawo nye Xoritɔwo ƒe amegãwo le Seir-nyigba la dzi le woƒe duawo nu.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau took their names from their families and the places where they lived. They are Timna, Alvah, Jetheth,
text: Esiawo nye amegã, siwo dzɔ tso Esau me la, ƒe ŋkɔwo le woƒe ƒomewo, nɔƒewo kple ŋkɔwo nu. Amegã Timna, amegã Alwa, amegã Yetet,

ref: Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon,
text: amegã Oholibama, amegã Ela, amegã Pinon,

ref: Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar,
text: amegã Kenas, amegã Teman, amegã Mibzar,

ref: Magdiel, and Iram. These clans descended from Esau, who was known as Edom, the father of the Edomites. They took their names from the places where they settled.
text: amegã Magdiel kple amegã Iram. Esiawo nye Edom-megãwo le woƒe nɔƒewo kple woawo ŋutɔwo ƒe anyigbawonu. Eya nye Esau, amesi nye Edomtɔwo ƒe fofo.

ref: Jacob lived in the land of Canaan, where his father Isaac had lived,
text: Ke Yakob nɔ anyigba, si dzi fofoa nye amedzro le la dzi, si enye Kanaan-nyigba la.

ref: “They're not here anymore,” the man replied. “I overheard them say they were going to Dothan.” Joseph left and found his brothers in Dothan.
text: Ŋutsu la gblɔ bena: Mese wogblɔ bena: Mina míaʋu le afisia ayi Dotan! Tete Yosef li nɔviawo, eye wòva tu wo le Dotan.

ref: Jacob loved Joseph more than he did any of his other sons, because Joseph was born when Jacob was very old. Jacob had even given Joseph a fancy coat
text: Eye Israel lɔ̃ Yosef wu viaŋutsu bubuawo katã, elabena edzii le eƒe amegãkuku me, eye wòtɔ awu ʋlaya nɛ.

ref: which showed that Joseph was his favorite son, and so Joseph's brothers hated him and would not be friendly to him.
text: Ke esi nɔviawo kpɔ bena, wo fofo lɔ̃nɛ wu wo katã la, wolée ɖe fu me, eye womegaƒoa nu nɛ nyuie o.

ref: One day, Joseph told his brothers what he had dreamed, and they hated him even more.
text: Gbe ɖeka Yosef ku drɔ̃e, eye wòlĩi na nɔviawo; tso ekema dzi la wogale fui ɖe dzi.

ref: Joseph said, “Let me tell you about my dream.
text: Egblɔ na wo bena: Miɖo to, ne miase drɔ̃e, si meku!

ref: We were out in the field, tying up bundles of wheat. Suddenly my bundle stood up, and your bundles gathered around and bowed down to it.”
text: Miele mɔli biam le agble me, tete nye mɔli babla tsi tre, eye wòdzɔ kã; kasiaa mia tɔwo va ƒo xlãe, eye wode la agu nɛ.

ref: His brothers asked, “Do you really think you are going to be king and rule over us?” Now they hated Joseph more than ever because of what he had said about his dream.
text: Tete nɔviawo gblɔ nɛ bena: Ɖe nèle didim be, naɖu fia ɖe mía nu, eye naɖu mía dzia? Tso ekema dzi wogalé fui ɖe dzi wu le eƒe drɔ̃ekuku kple eƒe nyawo ta.

ref: Joseph later had another dream, and he told his brothers, “Listen to what else I dreamed. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
text: Gbe ɖeka egaku drɔ̃e bubu, eye woli eya hã na nɔviawo bena: Megaku drɔ̃e be, ɣe, ɣleti kple ɣletivi wuiɖekɛ wode ta agu nam.

ref: When he told his father about this dream, his father became angry and said, “What's that supposed to mean? Are your mother and I and your brothers all going to come and bow down to you?”
text: Eye esi wòlii na fofoa kple nɔviawo la, fofoa ka mo nɛ, eye wògblɔ nɛ bena: Drɔ̃e ka enye si nèku? Alo ɖe mia kple dawo kple nɔviwòwo míava dze klo ade ta agu na wò mahã?

ref: Joseph's brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept wondering about the dream. Joseph Is Sold and Taken to Egypt
text: Tso esia dzi la nɔviawo ʋã ŋui; ke fofoa dzra nya la ɖo. Woƒle Yosef yi ɖe Egipte.

ref: One day when Joseph's brothers had taken the sheep to a pasture near Shechem,
text: Gbe ɖeka esi nɔviawo yi wo fofo ƒe alẽwo kplɔ ge le Sixem la,

ref: his father Jacob said to him, “I want you to go to your brothers. They are with the sheep near Shechem.” “Yes, sir,” Joseph answered.
text: Israel gblɔ na Yosef bena: Nɔviwòwo le alẽwo kplɔm le Sixem, va madɔ wò ɖe wo gbɔ. Eɖo ŋu bena: Nye enye si!

ref: His father said, “Go and find out how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and let me know.” So he sent him from Hebron Valley. Joseph was near Shechem
text: Eye wògblɔ ne bena: Yi ɖakpɔ bena, nɔviwòwo kple lãhawo li sesĩe mahã? Emegbe la nava gblɔe nam. Ale wòdɔe tso Xebron-bali me, eye wòva Sixem.

ref: and wandering through the fields, when a man asked, “What are you looking for?”
text: Ke esi wòtra mɔ le gbedzi la, ŋutsua ɖe kpɔe, eye wòbiae bena: Nuka dim nèle?

ref: Joseph answered, “I'm looking for my brothers who are watching the sheep. Can you tell me where they are?”
text: Eɖo ŋu bena: Nɔvinyewo dim mele: mede kuku, gblɔ afisi wole woƒe lãhawo nyim le la nam.

ref: But before he got there, they saw him coming and made plans to kill him.
text: Ke esi wokpɔe le adzɔge, eye mekpɔ ɖo wo gbɔ haɖe o la, wode adaŋu le eŋu be, yewoawui le aye me.

ref: They said to one another, “Look, here comes the hero of those dreams!
text: Eye wogblɔ na wo nɔewo bena: Mikpɔ drɔ̃ekula enye kem gbɔna ɖa!

ref: pieces of silver they sold him to the Ishmaelites who took him to Egypt.
text: Mina miawui, eye miatsɔe aƒu gbe ɖe ɖoa ɖe me, eye míagblɔ bena, lã wodaa ɖe vuvui, ekema miakpɔ nusi eƒe drɔ̃e la ava zu.

ref: Reuben heard this and tried to protect Joseph from them. “Let's not kill him,” he said.
text: Esi Ruben see la, exɔ nya ɖe enu gblɔ bena: Gbeɖe, migana miawui o!

ref: “Don't murder him or even harm him. Just throw him into a well out here in the desert.” Reuben planned to rescue Joseph later and take him back to his father.
text: Tete Ruben gblɔ na wo bena: Migakɔ ʋua ɖeke ɖi o: ke boŋ mitsɔe de do kema, si le gbedzi la me, eye migade asi eŋu o! Elabena ele didim be, yeaxɔe le wo si, eye yeaɖoe ɖe fofoa.

ref: When Joseph came to his brothers, they pulled off his fancy coat
text: Esi Yosef va nɔviawo gbɔ la, woɖe eƒe awu le eŋu, eƒe awu ʋlaya, si wòɖo.

ref: As Joseph's brothers sat down to eat, they looked up and saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead. Their camels were loaded with all kinds of spices that they were taking to Egypt.
text: Emegbe wobɔbɔ na anyi, be yewoaɖu nu, kasiaa wokpɔ Ismael-sihawo tso Gilead gbɔna heyina ɖe Egipte, eye woƒe kposɔwo tsɔ aŋe, lifi kple kotoklobo.

ref: So Judah said, “What will we gain if we kill our brother and hide his body?
text: Tete Yuda golɔ na nɔviawo bena: Viɖe ka wònye na mi, ne míefi mía nɔvi wu?

ref: Let's sell him to the Ishmaelites and not harm him. After all, he is our brother.” And the others agreed.
text: Mina míatsɔe adzra na Ismaeltɔwo, ke mígade asi eŋu o, elabena mía nɔvi kpamkpam wònye. Eye nɔviawo lɔ̃ ɖe edzi.

ref: When the Midianite merchants came by, Joseph's brothers took him out of the well, and for
text: Ke Midian-sitsalawo va to wo ŋuti yi, eye woɖe Yosef le do la me hetsɔ dzra na Ismaeltɔwo klosalo blave, eye wokplɔ Yosef yi Egipte.

ref: When Reuben returned to the well and did not find Joseph there, he tore his clothes in sorrow.
text: Esi Ruben gatrɔ va doa to la, megakpɔ Yosef le do la me o Tete wòdze eƒe awu,

ref: Then he went back to his brothers and said, “The boy is gone! What am I going to do?”
text: eye esi wògatrɔ va nɔviawo gbɔ la, egblɔ bena: Ɖevia megali o; azɔ afika nye la mayi? Yakob fa konyi le Yosef ŋuti.

ref: Joseph's brothers killed a goat and dipped Joseph's fancy coat in its blood.
text: Emegbe la wowu gbɔ̃tsua ɖe, eye wotso Yosef ƒe awu la nyrɔ ɖe ʋua me.

ref: After this, they took the coat to their father and said, “We found this! Look at it carefully and see if it belongs to your son.”
text: Eye wotso awu ʋlaya la ɖo ɖe wo fofo gblɔ bena: Nusia míefɔ, le ŋku ɖe ŋu nyuie, be viwò ƒe awue loo, alo menye eyae o!

ref: Jacob knew it was Joseph's coat and said, “It's my son's coat! Joseph has been torn to pieces and eaten by some wild animal.”
text: Esi wòɖe ŋku ɖee nyuie la, edo ɣli bena: Vinye ƒe awu enye esia; lã wodaa ɖe vuvui, oo, evuvu Yosef vavã!

ref: Jacob mourned for Joseph a long time, and to show his sorrow he tore his clothes and wore sackcloth.
text: Tete Yakob dze eƒe awu, sa papahũ ɖe ali, eye wofa konyi na via ŋkeke geɖewo.

ref: All of Jacob's children came to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted. “No,” he said, “I will go to my grave, mourning for my son.” So Jacob kept on grieving.
text: Eye viaŋutsuwo kple vianyɔnuwo katã la akɔ nɛ ʋuu do kpoe, ke egblɔ bena: Matsɔ konyifafa ayi vinye gbɔe le tsieƒe boŋ. Ale fofoa fa nɛ ŋutɔ.

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Ke Midiantɔwo ɖadzrae ɖe Egipte na Potifar, Farao ƒe aƒedzikpɔla kple eŋuti mewo ƒe amegã.

ref: About that time Judah left his brothers in the hill country and went to live near his friend Hirah in the town of Adullam.
text: Le ɣemaɣi me la Yuda ʋu le nɔviawo gbɔ heyi ɖe Xira. Adulamtɔ la gbɔ.

ref: While there he met the daughter of Shua, a Canaanite man. Judah married her,
text: Afima Yuda kpo Kanaantɔ Sua ƒe vinyɔnu le, eye woɖee, eye wòdo kplii.

ref: and they had three sons. He named the first one Er;
text: Eye wòfa fu hedzi ŋutsuvi, eye wòna ŋkɔe bena Ger.

ref: she named the next one Onan.
text: Eyome la efɔ fu gadzi ŋutsuvi, eye wòna ŋkɔe be Onan.

ref: The third one was born when Judah was in Chezib, and she named him Shelah.
text: Emegbe la egadzi ŋutsuvi, eye wòna ŋkɔe bena Sela. Enɔ Kesib hafi dzii.

ref: Later, Judah chose Tamar as a wife for Er, his oldest son.
text: Eye Yuda ɖe srɔ̃ na eƒe ŋgɔgbevi Ger, eŋku nye Tamar.

ref: But Er was very evil, and the Lord took his life.
text: Ke Yuda ƒe ŋgɔgbevi Ger enye ame vɔ̃ɖi le Yehowa ƒe ŋku me, eyata Yehowa wui.

ref: So Judah told Onan, “It's your duty to marry Tamar and have a child for your brother.”
text: Tete Yuda gblɔ na Onan bena: Ɖe nɔviwò srɔ̃ ahoe, bena nado ƒome ɖi na nɔviwò.

ref: Onan knew the child would not be his, and when he had sex with Tamar, he made sure that she would not get pregnant.
text: Ke esi Onan nya bena, ɖeviawo manye ye tɔ o la, ɖaa ne edɔ nɔvia srɔ̃ gbɔ la, enana tsi la duduna ɖe anyigba, bena yemagado ƒome ɖi na nɔvia o.

ref: The Lord wasn't pleased with Onan and took his life too.
text: Nuwɔwɔ sia vɔ̃ɖi le Yehowa ƒe ŋku me, eyata wowu eya hã.

ref: Judah did not want the same thing to happen to his son Shelah, and he told Tamar, “Go home to your father and live there as a widow until my son Shelah is grown.” So Tamar went to live with her father.
text: Eye Yuda gblɔ na lɔ̃xoa Tamar bena: Yi fofowò ƒe me, eye nanye ahosi, vaseɖe esime vinye Sela natsi. Elabena ele vɔvɔ̃m bena, eya hã ava ku abe nɔviawo ke ene. Tete Tamar yi ɖanɔ fofoa ƒe me.

ref: Some years later Judah's wife died, and he mourned for her. He then went with his friend Hirah to the town of Timnah, where his sheep were being sheared.
text: Le ŋkeke geɖewo megbe la Sua vinyɔnu, si nye Yuda srɔ̃ la ku. Eye esi Yuda fa nɛ vɔ la, eya kple xɔlɔ̃a Xira, Adulamtɔ la woyi ɖe Timnat, be woakpɔ amesiwo le fu sẽm na eƒe alẽwo la ɖa.

ref: Tamar found out that her father-in-law Judah was going to Timnah to shear his sheep.
text: Ke esi wogblɔ na Tamar bena, toa gbɔna va Timnat fu sẽ ge na eƒe alẽwo la,

ref: She also realized that Shelah was now a grown man, but she had not been allowed to marry him. So she decided to dress in something other than her widow's clothes and to cover her face with a veil. After this, she sat outside the town of Enaim on the road to Timnah.
text: eɖe eƒe ahovɔwo ɖi, tsɔ motsyɔnu tsyɔ ŋku me, eye wòtsyɔ avɔ blukɔɔ heva bɔbɔ nɔ Enaim-gbo, si le Titmnat-mɔ dzi la nu; elabena ekpɔ be, Sela tsi, gake wometsɔ ye nɛ, be wòaɖe o.

ref: When Judah came along, he did not recognize her because of the veil. He thought she was a prostitute
text: Ke esi Yuda kpɔe la, ebu be, gboloa ɖee; elabena etsyɔ nu ŋku me.

ref: and asked her to sleep with him. She asked, “What will you give me if I do?”
text: Tete wòdze yi ɖe egbɔ gblɔ bena: Na mamlɔ gbɔwò! Elabena menyae bena, lɔ̃xoa wònye o. Eɖo ŋu nɛ bena: Nuka nanam hafi amlɔ gbɔnye?

ref: “One of my young goats,” he answered. “What will you give me to keep until you send the goat?” she asked.
text: Egblɔ bena: Malé gbɔ̃tsu ɖeka le lãhawo me aɖo ɖe wò. Eɖo ŋu bena: Ekema tsɔ nane ɖo awɔba nam, vaseɖe esime naɖoe ɖem.

ref: “What do you want?” he asked in return. “The ring on that cord around your neck,” was her reply. “I also want the special walking stick you have with you.” He gave them to her, they slept together, and she became pregnant.
text: Eye wòbiae bena: Nuka matsɔ aɖo awɔba na wò mahã? Eɖo ŋu be: Wò ŋkɔsigɛ, si nètsɔna ɖoa ŋkɔ nu dzi, kple eŋuti ka kpakple wò atikplɔ, si le asiwò la! Tete wotsɔ wo nɛ, eye wòmia egbɔ, eye wòfɔ lu nɛ.

ref: After returning home, Tamar took off the veil and dressed in her widow's clothes again.
text: Tete wòdzo yi, eye wòɖe motsyɔnu la ɖi gatsɔ eƒe ahovɔwo ta.

ref: Judah asked his friend Hirah take a goat to the woman, so he could get back the ring and walking stick, but she wasn't there.
text: Emegbe la Yuda tsɔ gbɔ̃tsu la ɖe xɔlɔ̃a Adulamtɔ la si, be wòaxɔ awɔbaɖonu la le nyɔnu la si; gake mekpɔe o.

ref: Hirah asked the people of Enaim, “Where is the prostitute who sat along the road outside your town?” “There's never been one here,” they answered.
text: Eye wòbia dua me ŋutsuwo bena: Afika gbolo, si nɔ Enaim-mɔ dzi la le? Woɖo eŋu ne bena: Gboloa ɖeke menɔ afisia kpɔ o.

ref: Hirah went back and told Judah, “I couldn't find the woman, and the people of Enaim said no prostitute had ever been there.”
text: Tete wòtrɔ yi Yuda gbɔ gblɔ bena: Nyemekpɔe o, eye dua me ŋutsuwo hã gblɔ bena, gboloa ɖeke menɔ afisia kpɔ o.

ref: “If you couldn't find her, we'll just let her keep the things I gave her,” Judah answered. “And we'd better forget about the goat, or else we'll look like fools.”
text: Yuda gblɔ bena: Nenɔ esi ko! Ne menya ɖu fewu le mía ŋuti o ko. Meɖo gbɔ̃tsu la ɖee, ke mèkpɔe o.

ref: About three months later someone told Judah, “Your daughter-in-law Tamar has behaved like a prostitute, and now she's pregnant!” “Drag her out of town and burn her to death!” Judah shouted.
text: Anɔ ɣleti etɔ̃ megbe la wogblɔ na Yuda bena: Lɔ̃xowò Tamar wɔ ahasi, eye wòfɔ fu ɖe eme. Tete Yuda gblɔ bena: Mikplɔe ɖo goe, ne míatɔ dzoe!

ref: As Tamar was being dragged off, she sent someone to tell her father-in-law, “The man who gave me this ring, this cord, and this walking stick is the one who got me pregnant.”
text: Ke esi wokplɔe do goe la, edɔ ame ɖo ɖe toa gbɔ gblɔ bena: Ŋutsu, si ƒe nu enye esiawo la, eyae ɖo fum. Eye wògblɔ bena: Lé ŋku ɖe eŋu nyuie be, ameka tɔ enye ŋkɔsigɛ la kple eŋuti ka kpakple alikplɔ la mahã?

ref: “Those are mine!” Judah admitted. “She's a better person than I am, because I broke my promise to let her marry my son Shelah.” After this, Judah never slept with her again.
text: Esi Yuda ɖe ŋku ɖee nyuie la, egblɔ bena: Etɔ dzɔ wu tanye, elabena nyematsɔe na vinye Sela wòɖe o. Tso ema dzi la megade asi eŋu kpɔ o.

ref: Tamar later gave birth to twins. But before either of them was born, one of them stuck a hand out of her womb. The woman who was helping tied a red thread around the baby's hand and explained, “This one came out first.”
text: Esime wòle vi dzim la, ɖeka do asi ɖa. Tete vixela tsɔ ka dzia ɖe sa ɖe asi ŋu nɛ gblɔ bena: Esiae tre dodo.

ref: At once his hand went back in, and the other child was born first. The woman then said, “What an opening you've made for yourself!” So they named the baby Perez.
text: Ke esi wògahe asi ɖe eme la, nɔvia do, eye vixela gblɔ bena: Nukaŋuti nèdze le nɔviwò ŋu ale? Eyata wòna ŋkɔe bena: Perez.

ref: When the brother with the red thread was born, they named him Zerah.
text: Emegbe la nɔvia, si ƒe asi ŋu ka dzi le la, hã do; eyata wona ŋkɔe bena Serax.

ref: The Ishmaelites took Joseph to Egypt and sold him to Potiphar, the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Eye esi wokplɔ Yosef va Egipte la, Egiptetɔ Potifar, Farao ƒe aƒedzikpɔla kple eŋuti mewo ƒe amegã la ƒlee le Ismaeltɔ, siwo kplɔe yi afima la si.

ref: So Joseph lived in the home of Potiphar, his Egyptian owner. Soon Potiphar realized that the Lord was helping Joseph to be successful in whatever he did.
text: Esi eƒe aƒetɔ kpɔ bena, Yehowa li kplii, eye Yehowa na nusianu, si wòwɔna, dzea edzi nɛ la,

ref: Potiphar liked Joseph and made him his personal assistant, putting him in charge of his house and all of his property.
text: eve Yosef nu, eye wòtsɔe wɔ eƒe subɔlae, eye wòtsɔe ɖo eƒe me nu wo dzikpɔlae hetsɔ eƒe nuwo katã ɖe esi.

ref: Potiphar left everything up to Joseph, and with Joseph there, the only decision he had to make was what he wanted to eat. Joseph was well-built and handsome,
text: Eye wotsɔ eƒe nuwo katã de Yosef si, eye megatsɔ ɖeke le nyaa ɖeke me o, negbe eƒe nuɖuɖu ko. Eye Yosef nyo, eye wòdze ɖeka ŋutɔ. Yosef nɔ anyi dzadzɛ.

ref: and Potiphar's wife soon noticed him. She asked him to make love to her,
text: Le nusiawo megbe la eƒe aƒetɔ srɔ̃ nɔa ahiãmoe ɖom ɖe Yosef ŋu, eye wògblɔ bena: Va mlɔ gbɔnye.

ref: but he refused and said, “My master isn't worried about anything in his house, because he has placed me in charge of everything he owns.
text: Ke egbe, eye wògblɔ na eƒe aƒetɔ srɔ̃ bena: Kpɔ ɖa, nye aƒetɔ megatsɔ ɖeke le aƒea ƒe nya me o, eye wòtsɔ eƒe nuwo katã ɖe asi nam.

ref: No one in my master's house is more important than I am. The only thing he hasn't given me is you, and that's because you are his wife. I won't sin against God by doing such a terrible thing as this.”
text: Amea ɖeke meɖe nunye le aƒe sia me o, eye womeɖe naneke le eme nam o, negbe wò ko, elabena srɔ̃a nènye. Aleke gɔ̃ mawɔ vɔ̃ gã sia, eye mada vo ɖe Mawu ŋuti mahã?

ref: She kept begging Joseph day after day, but he refused to do what she wanted or even to go near her.
text: Eye wonɔa nya sia gblɔm na Yosef ŋkeke sia ŋkeke, ke melɔ̃ be, yeamlɔ egbɔ, eye yeade asi eŋu o.

ref: One day, Joseph went to Potiphar's house to do his work, and none of the other servants were there.
text: Gbe ɖeka esi wòva aƒea me, be wòawɔ eƒe dɔwo, eye aƒeametɔa ɖeke meli o la,

ref: Potiphar's wife grabbed hold of his coat and said, “Make love to me!” Joseph ran out of the house, leaving his coat there in her hands.
text: ele eƒe avɔ, eye wògblɔ bena: Va mlɔ anyi kplim! Ke egblẽ eƒe avɔ la ɖe esi me, eye wòsi do go le aƒea me.

ref: she called in her servants and said, “Look! This Hebrew has come just to make fools of us. He tried to rape me, but I screamed for help.
text: eya aƒeametɔwo vɛ, eye wògblɔ na wo bena: Mikpɔ ɖa, ekplɔ Hebritɔ sia vɛ na mi, amesi ɖi be, yeado ŋukpe mí. Eva gbɔnye, be yeamlɔ anyi kplim, ke medo ɣli sesĩe!

ref: And when he heard me scream, he ran out of the house, leaving his coat with me.”
text: Esi wòkpɔ be, medo ɣli sesĩe la, egblẽ eƒe avɔ ɖe gbɔnye, eye wòsi do go le aƒea me.

ref: Potiphar's wife kept Joseph's coat until her husband came home.
text: Eye wotsɔ eƒe avɔ ta da ɖe eɖokui gbɔ, vaseɖe esime eƒe aƒetɔ gbɔ.

ref: Then she said, “That Hebrew slave of yours tried to rape me!
text: Eye wògblɔ nya mawo ke na eya hã bena: Kpɔ ɖa, Hebri-kluvi, si nekplɔ vɛ na mi la, va gbɔnye, be yeado ŋukpem.

ref: But when I screamed for help, he left his coat and ran out of the house.”
text: Ke esi medo ɣli sesĩe la, egblẽ eƒe avɔ ɖe gbɔnye, eye wòsi do go le aƒea me. Wode Yosef gaxɔ me.

ref: and threw Joseph in the same prison where the king's prisoners were kept. While Joseph was in prison,
text: Eye Yosef ƒe aƒetɔ lée de gaxɔ, si me fia ƒe gatɔwo nɔna la me, eye wònɔ afima le gaxɔa me.

ref: the Lord helped him and was good to him. He even made the jailer like Joseph so much that
text: Ke Yehowa nɔ kple Yosef, eye wòna gaxɔdzikpɔla ƒe dzime kɔ ɖe eŋu, eye wòve enu.

ref: he put him in charge of the other prisoners and of everything that was done in the jail.
text: Eye gaxɔdzikpɔla tsɔ gatɔ, siwo katã le gaxɔa me la, de Yosef si, eye nusianu, si wowɔna le afima la, eyae ɖonɛ na wo.

ref: The jailer did not worry about anything, because the Lord was with Joseph and made him successful in all that he did.
text: Eye gaxɔdzikpɔla metsɔ ɖeke le nusi wòwɔna la me o, elabena Yehowa li kplii, eye nusianu, si wòwɔ na la Yehowa na wòdzea edzi nɛ.

ref: While Joseph was in prison, both the king's personal servant and his chief cook made the king angry. So he had them thrown into the same prison with Joseph.
text: Eye wotsɔ wo de gaxɔ, si le fia ŋuti mewo ƒe amegã ƒe xɔ, si me wode Yosef hãe la me.

ref: They spent a long time in prison, and the official in charge of the palace guard, made Joseph their servant.
text: Fia ŋuti mewo ƒe amegã la tsɔ wo de Yosef si, bena wòakpɔ wo dzi, eye wonɔ gaxɔ me ɣeyiɣi didia ɖe.

ref: One night each of the two men had a dream, but their dreams had different meanings.
text: Gbe ɖeka ƒe zã Egipte-fia ƒe ahakula kple aboloƒola, siwo le gaxɔ me la, woku drɔ̃e wo tɔ wo tɔwoe, eye drɔ̃eawo gɔme le vovovo.

ref: The next morning, when Joseph went to see the men, he could tell they were upset,
text: Esi Yosef va wo gbɔ le ŋdi la, ekpɔ wo bena, woli kpoo.

ref: “We each had a dream last night,” they answered, “and there is no one to tell us what they mean.” Joseph replied, “Doesn't God know the meaning of dreams? Now tell me what you dreamed.”
text: Woɖo eŋu nɛ bena: Míeku drɔ̃e, gake amesi aɖe egɔme, meli o. Tete Yosef gblɔ na wo bena: Alo drɔ̃e gɔmeɖeɖe la menye Mawu fɔe oa? Gake milii nam ko! Ahakula la ƒe drɔ̃ekuku.

ref: The king's personal servant told Joseph, “In my dream I saw a vine
text: Tete ahakulawo ƒe amegã lĩ eƒe drɔ̃e la na Yosef, eye wògblɔ nɛ bena: Meku drɔ̃e kpɔ weinkaa ɖe le ŋkunye me.

ref: with three branches. As soon as it budded, it blossomed, and its grapes became ripe.
text: Alɔ etɔ̃ le weinka sia ŋu, eye esi wode asi dzedze me la, seƒoƒo ɖo wo dzi, eye woƒe tsetsewo de asi ɖiɖi me.

ref: I held the king's cup and squeezed the grapes into it, then I gave the cup to the king.”
text: Ke Farao ƒe kplu la le asinye; tete megbe weinkpoawo, eye mefiã wo ɖe Farao ƒe kplu la me, emegbe metsɔ kplu la de asi na Farao.

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three branches stand for three days,
text: Yosef ɖo ŋu nɛ bena: Drɔ̃e la gɔme enye si: Alɔ etɔ̃awo nye ŋkeke etɔ̃.

ref: and in three days the king will pardon you. He will make you his personal servant again, and you will serve him his wine, just as you used to do.
text: Le ŋkeke etɔ̃agbe la Farao ado wò ɖe dzi, eye wòagatsɔ wò aɖo wò dɔ nu. Eye nagatsɔ kplu ade Farao si, abe alesi nèwɔna tsã, esi nenye eƒe ahakula ene.

ref: But when these good things happen, please don't forget to tell the king about me, so I can get out of this place.
text: Ke ɖo ŋku dzinye, ne eva nyo na wò! Meɖe kuku, ve nunye, eye naɖo ŋku dzinye na Farao, bena wòaɖem le xɔ sia me.

ref: I was kidnapped from the land of the Hebrews, and here in Egypt I haven't done anything to deserve being thrown in jail.
text: Elabena yakatsyɔe wofim le Hebritɔwo ƒe anyigba dzi, eye afi sia hã nyemewɔ naneke, hafi wolém de ga o. Aboloƒola ƒe drɔ̃ekuku.

ref: When the chief cook saw that Joseph had given a good meaning to the dream, he told Joseph, “I also had a dream. In it I was carrying three breadbaskets stacked on top of my head.
text: Esi aboloƒolawo ƒe amegã kpɔ bena, drɔ̃e la gɔme nyo la, egblɔ na Yosef bena: Nye hã meku drɔ̃e, eye metsɔ abolo kusi etɔ̃ ɖe ta.

ref: The top basket was full of all kinds of baked things for the king, but birds were eating them.”
text: Ke kusi etametɔ me la abolo vivi vovovowo li na Farao, eye xeviwo va le wo ɖum de kusia me le tanye.

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three baskets are three days,
text: Yosef ɖo ŋu nɛ bena: Drɔ̃e la gɔme enye si: Kusi etɔ̃awo nye ŋkeke etɔ̃.

ref: and in three days the king will cut off your head. He will hang your body on a pole, and birds will come and peck at it.
text: Le ŋkeke etɔ̃agbe la Farao atso ta le nuwo, eye woahe wò ɖe ati ŋu, eye xeviwo ava ɖu wò ŋutilã. Alesi drɔ̃ewo va ve me.

ref: Three days later, while the king was celebrating his birthday with a dinner for his officials, he sent for his personal servant and the chief cook.
text: Le ŋkeke etɔ̃agbe la Farao ɖu eƒe dzigbe, eye wòɖo kplɔ̃ na eƒe dɔlawo katã, eye wòɖe ahakulawo ƒe amegã kple aboloƒolawo ƒe amegã le gaxɔa me le eƒe dɔlawo katã ƒe ŋku me.

ref: He put the personal servant back in his old job
text: Egatsɔ ahakulawo ƒe amegã la ɖo eƒe ahakudɔ nu, bena wòagatsɔ kplu anɔ asi ɖem na Farao.

ref: and had the cook put to death. Everything happened just as Joseph had said it would,
text: Ke ena wohe aboloƒolawo ƒe amegã ya ɖe ati ŋu, abe alesi Yosef ɖe egɔme na wo ene.

ref: but the king's personal servant completely forgot about Joseph.
text: Ahakulawo ƒe amegã megaɖo ŋku Yosef dzi o, ke boŋ eŋlɔe be.

ref: Two years later the king of Egypt dreamed he was standing beside the Nile River.
text: Le ƒe eve megbe la Farao ku drɔ̃e. Ele tsitre ɖe Nil to.

ref: Suddenly, seven fat, healthy cows came up from the river and started eating grass along the bank.
text: Eye wòkpɔ nyinɔ adre, siwo nyo, eye woda ami la, do go tso tɔsisi la me, eye wole gbe ɖum le tagba.

ref: ate the fat, healthy cows. When this happened, the king woke up.
text: Eye nyinɔ, siwo vlo, eye woɖi ku nyatsã la, lé nyinɔ adre, siwo nyo, eye woda ami la mi. Tete Farao nyo.

ref: The king went back to sleep and had another dream. This time seven full heads of grain were growing on a single stalk.
text: Esi wògayi alo me la, egaku drɔ̃e, eye wòkpɔ liŋɔ adre, siwo ʋã, eye wodo ku la, do ɖe luti ɖeka ŋu.

ref: Later, seven other heads of grain appeared, but they were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: Wo yome la liŋɔ adre, siwo pepi lé, eye wòlé bu la do.

ref: The thin heads of grain swallowed the seven full heads. Again the king woke up, and it had only been a dream.
text: Eye liŋɔ, siwo lé bu la, mi liŋɔ adre, siwo ʋã, eye wodo ku la. Esi Farao nyo la, edze sii bena, drɔ̃e yeku.

ref: The next morning the king was upset. So he called in his magicians and wise men and told them what he had dreamed. None of them could tell him what the dreams meant.
text: Le ŋdi la Farao mekpɔ gbɔdzɔea ɖeke o, eye wòdo woyi ɖayɔ Egipte-fakalawo kple nunyalawo va ƒo ƒu, eye Farao li drɔ̃eawo na wo; ke wo dometɔa ɖeke mete ŋu ɖe egɔme na Farao o.

ref: The king's personal servant said: Now I remember what I was supposed to do.
text: Tete ahakulawo ƒe amegã ƒo nu kple Farao gblɔ bena: Egbe la meɖo ŋku nye vodada dzi.

ref: When you were angry with me and your chief cook, you threw us both in jail in the house of the captain of the guard.
text: Esi Farao bi dzi ɖe eƒe dɔlawo ŋuti, eye wòna wòlé mía kple aboloƒolawo ƒe amegã de gaxɔ me le fia ŋuti mewo ƒe amegã ƒe me la,

ref: One night we both had dreams, and each dream had a different meaning.
text: mía kple eve míeku drɔ̃e le zã ɖeka me mia ta míatɔe, eye drɔ̃eawo gɔme hã le vovovo.
